qualification_assessment.qualification_basis: Multiple terms name humility as an explicit inner disposition of the person: tapeinofrosunē (lowliness of mind, Phil 2:3), a.na.vah (inner gentleness/humility, Pro 15:33; 22:4), a.nav (the meek/humble as a named category of persons before God). The term set spans the full inner-being range — the settled disposition of lowliness, the active exercise of humility in relational conduct, and humility as the condition that God responds to with grace (Jam 4:6; 1Pe 5:5). Christ is explicitly named as the model of the inner state: 'learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart' (tapeinofrosunē/tapeinos, Mat 11:29).

term_inventory.meaning_summary: Humility, gentleness, meekness — the inner disposition of lowliness. Derived from a.nav (H6035, the humble person). The source file lists a.na.vah (עֲנָוָה) and an.vah (עַנְוָה) as separate entries but both carry H6038 and are variant spellings of the same lemma. an.vah is flagged 'Exclude word This word only' in STEP — treated here as a variant form consolidated under a.na.vah.

Humble, meek, poor, afflicted — the person characterised by lowliness before God. Covers both the humble inner disposition (Psa 25:9 — God leads the humble) and the social condition of the poor/afflicted (Job 24:4; Amo 2:7). The inner-being sub-sense is the person whose spirit is lowly and who looks to God; the socioeconomic sub-sense is the materially poor. These overlap but are distinct.
Modest, humble — unassuming, reserved. Single occurrence: Pro 11:2. The root tsa.na (to be modest/reserved) gives this term a behavioural-discretion dimension — humility as self-contained restraint.

Humility of mind, lowliness — the inner disposition of considering others more significant than oneself (Phil 2:3). Compound: tapeinos (lowly) + phren (mind/disposition). The NT's primary term for the inner disposition of humility. Note: standard spelling is tapeinophrosunē; the source file uses tapeinofrosunē consistently.

Lowly, humble, of low degree — used of persons in low social position and of the inner disposition of lowliness. Christ applies it to himself: 'I am gentle and lowly (tapeinos) in heart' (Mat 11:29). God gives grace to the tapeinos (Jam 4:6; 1Pe 5:5). The Magnificat uses it of those God exalts (Luk 1:52).

Meek, gentle — power or strength held under control. Not weakness but controlled restraint. Applied to Christ (Mat 11:29; 21:5) and to the blessed (Mat 5:5). LSJ: gentle, mild, tame — but in NT context carries the sense of strength restrained in service of others.

Gentleness, meekness — the noun form of praus. Appears in paraenetic (ethical instruction) contexts. Data quality note: the source file lists prautēs (G4236) twice with identical verse lists — both occurrences are the same term block repeated. praotēs (G4240) is a variant spelling of the same word listed separately.

Gentleness, meekness — variant spelling of prautēs (G4236). Both G4236 and G4240 name the same quality. The distinction in the source file reflects manuscript spelling variation rather than a semantic difference. Fruit of the Spirit (Gal 5:23); spirit of gentleness in correction (Gal 6:1; 2Ti 2:25); the gentleness of Christ (2Cor 10:1).

related_words.root_relationship: same root cluster — affliction that produces humility

verse_classification.reason: your gentleness made me great — divine gentleness/humility as the source of the psalmist's elevation; the inner quality of God as the cause of human flourishing
humility comes before honour — a.na.vah as the inner disposition that precedes and enables divine honour
humility comes before honour — direct pairing of a.na.vah with the outcome of honour; inner disposition named as precondition
the reward for humility and fear of the Lord — a.na.vah named alongside the fear of the Lord as the combined inner-dispositional base for riches, honour, and life
seek humility — a.na.vah as a quality to be actively sought; the humble are addressed as a named community before God
your gentleness made me great — parallel text of 2Sa 22:36; an.vah variant spelling; same inner quality of divine gentleness
ride out victoriously for the cause of truth and meekness and righteousness — a.na.vah/an.vah as a named quality of the royal warrior; humility as an inner disposition in the exercise of power
Moses was very meek, more than all people — the definitive OT inner-being statement about humility as a named character quality of a person
He leads the humble in what is right and teaches the humble his way — a.nav as the inner disposition that opens a person to divine guidance
let the humble hear and be glad — a.nav as the inner state that makes the person receptive to good news from God
the meek shall inherit the land — a.nav as the named inner disposition whose outcome is inheritance; the eschatological promise of the humble
when the humble see it they will be glad — a.nav as the receptive inner state that registers God's action with joy
to save all the humble of the earth — a.nav as the inner disposition that defines those who are saved by divine judgment
the Lord lifts up the humble — a.nav as the inner disposition that elicits divine lifting-up
he adorns the humble with salvation — a.nav as the inner disposition that receives divine adornment
to the humble he gives favour — a.nav as the inner disposition that receives divine favour; paralleled by scorn given to scorners
better to be of a lowly spirit with the poor than to divide the spoil with the proud — a.nav/lowly spirit as the preferred inner disposition regardless of material outcome
with equity he shall decide for the meek of the earth — a.nav as the inner-dispositional category that receives the Messianic king's just judgment
the meek shall obtain fresh joy in the Lord — a.nav as the inner disposition whose eschatological outcome is joy in God
to bring good news to the poor/humble — a.nav as the primary recipient of the anointed one's proclamation; inner-dispositional humility as the condition of receptivity to gospel
poor of the earth hide — socioeconomic register; no inner humility disposition named
cry of the afflicted — social justice register; poor/afflicted as socioeconomic category
forget not the afflicted — poor as the oppressed social category
you hear the desire of the afflicted — a.nav as the inner state whose desire God attends to; the humility-before-God dimension is present alongside the affliction
the afflicted shall eat and be satisfied — a.nav as both the afflicted and the humble; those who seek God are parallel to the humble
generous to the poor — socioeconomic register; no inner humility disposition named
trample the head of the poor — social justice register; oppression of the materially poor
with the humble is wisdom — tsa.nu.a as the inner disposition paired with wisdom; the modest/reserved person as the one who holds wisdom
serving the Lord with all humility — tapeinofrosunē as the inner disposition governing apostolic service; the all (all humility) indicates totality of the inner posture
with all humility and gentleness — tapeinofrosunē as the first in the list of inner dispositions that enable relational unity; appears alongside praotēs in the same verse
in humility count others more significant than yourselves — tapeinofrosunē as the inner disposition directly defined: the cognitive-evaluative act of placing others above self
insisting on asceticism and worship of angels — false humility; tapeinofrosunē used of the appearance of humility that masks pride; the entry's negative case

appearance of wisdom in promoting self-made religion and asceticism and severity to the body — self-imposed tapeinofrosunē as no value; the inner disposition must be genuinely God-oriented not self-manufactured
put on humility, meekness, and patience — tapeinofrosunē as a garment to be deliberately worn; the inner disposition is cultivated, not merely received

clothe yourselves with humility toward one another — tapeinofrosunē as a relational garment; the inner disposition expressed toward each other; appears alongside tapeinos in the grace-to-the-humble quotation
blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth — praus as the inner disposition that receives the beatitude and the eschatological promise
your king is coming to you, humble, and mounted on a donkey — praus applied to Christ's triumphal entry; meekness as the defining quality of messianic kingship
pursue righteousness, godliness, faith, love, steadfastness, gentleness — prautēs in the pursuit list; inner disposition of meekness as a named quality to be actively pursued
the hidden person of the heart with the imperishable beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit — praus as the inner quality of the hidden person of the heart; explicitly inner-being location
to be gentle and to show perfect courtesy toward all people — prautēs as the inner disposition governing social conduct; gentleness toward all as the relational expression of the inner state
receive with meekness the implanted word — prautēs as the inner disposition that enables receptive reception of Scripture; humility as the condition of teachability
show his works in the meekness of wisdom — prautēs as the inner disposition from which genuine wisdom-works emerge; the fruit of wisdom is meekness in conduct
do it with gentleness and respect — prautēs as the inner disposition governing the manner of apologetic witness; the inner state shapes the quality of engagement with opponents
shall I come with love in a spirit of gentleness — praotēs as the inner disposition that determines the manner of apostolic presence; the choice of gentleness over severity is an inner-being choice
gentleness, self-control — praotēs as the ninth fruit of the Spirit; an inner disposition produced by the Spirit's activity
restore him in a spirit of gentleness — praotēs as the inner disposition governing restorative correction; the spirit of gentleness is the inner state of the one who corrects
with all humility and gentleness, with patience — praotēs as part of the triad of inner dispositions enabling relational unity; appears alongside tapeinofrosunē in the same verse
compassionate hearts, kindness, humility, meekness, and patience — praotēs as one of the five garment-virtues in the Colossians 3 list; an inner disposition to be deliberately clothed in
correcting his opponents with gentleness — praotēs as the inner disposition governing engagement with theological opponents; God may grant repentance through the gentleness of the corrector
I am gentle and lowly in heart — tapeinos applied to Christ's own inner state; the explicit heart (kardia) location makes this the entry's most direct inner-being statement about humility
exalted those of humble estate — tapeinos as the named condition of those who receive divine exaltation; the Magnificat's reversal of human hierarchy

do not be haughty, but associate with the lowly — tapeinos as the named category of people the non-haughty person associates with; the inner disposition of not being haughty is contrasted with the condition of the lowly
God, who comforts the downcast — tapeinos as the named state of those whom God specifically comforts; the lowly/downcast inner state is the condition of receptivity to divine comfort
I who am humble when face to face with you — tapeinos as a quality Paul claims of himself in person; the inner disposition of lowliness expressed in relational presence
let the lowly brother boast in his exaltation — tapeinos as the social-inner condition of the low-status believer who is exalted in Christ; the inner state and the social condition converge
God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble — tapeinos as the inner disposition that receives divine grace; the foundational statement of the entry's theological claim
God gives grace to the humble — tapeinos as the inner disposition that receives divine grace; parallel to Jam 4:6; appears alongside tapeinofrosunē in the same verse

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: a.na.vah / a.nav share root cluster with o.ni (affliction, H6040) and a.nah (to afflict/humble)
cross_registry_links.note: The Hebrew humility vocabulary is root-related to the affliction vocabulary. a.nav (the humble person) and a.ni/o.ni (the afflicted person) share the same ayin-nun root. The linguistic link suggests humility and grief-as-affliction are related inner states in the Hebrew conceptual field.

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: a.na.vah / tapeinos — explicitly contrasted with pride in Pro 15:33; 18:12; Jam 4:6; 1Pe 5:5
cross_registry_links.note: Humility and pride are the primary antonym pair across both Hebrew and Greek terms. Every major statement of humility's reward is paired with a statement about pride's fall or divine opposition.

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: praus / praotēs — meekness as controlled strength; anger as the force that meekness restrains
cross_registry_links.note: praus is characterised as power under control — the opposite of unrestrained expression. This directly connects the humility entry to the anger entry, where controlled vs uncontrolled inner states are the key distinction.

data_quality_flags.description: The source file lists a.na.vah (עֲנָוָה) and an.vah (עַנְוָה) as separate term blocks with note 'Exclude word This word only' on an.vah. Both carry H6038. an.vah is treated here as a variant spelling consolidated under a.na.vah. Psa 18:35 (an.vah) and 2Sa 22:36 (a.na.vah) are parallel texts of the same Davidic song with a variant spelling between them.

The source file contains two identical term blocks for prautēs with identical verse lists (Tit 3:2; Jam 1:21; Jam 3:13; 1Pe 3:15). These are treated as a single entry. prautēs (G4236) and praotēs (G4240) are also spelling variants of the same word listed as separate entries — both are kept as separate entries per source file structure.

Both a.na.vah and an.vah term blocks appear twice in the source file. The second occurrence of each is a STEP formatting repeat. The verse data is identical between both appearances. Deduplicated to single records.
Eph 4:2 appears under both tapeinofrosunē and praotēs — the verse contains both terms simultaneously. Separate records are kept under each term.
Col 3:12 appears under both tapeinofrosunē and praotēs — the verse contains both terms simultaneously. Separate records are kept under each term.
1Pe 5:5 appears under both tapeinofrosunē and tapeinos — the verse contains both terms simultaneously. Separate records are kept under each term.
Source file consistently spells this term tapeinofrosunē. Standard scholarly spelling is tapeinophrosunē (G5012). The source file spelling is used throughout as the anchor transliteration.
